name: Redis Cluster for Horizontal Scaling
description: Deploy a Redis Cluster with automatic data sharding and high availability across multiple nodes for production workloads.
prerequisites: At least 6 Redis instances (3 masters, 3 replicas)

steps.title: Prepare cluster configuration
steps.description: Enable cluster mode in each Redis instance configuration.
steps.command: redis-cli -p 7001 CONFIG SET cluster-enabled yes && redis-cli -p 7001 CONFIG SET cluster-config-file nodes-7001.conf && redis-cli -p 7001 CONFIG SET cluster-node-timeout 5000
steps.tip: Repeat for ports 7002-7006. Each node needs a unique cluster-config-file.

steps.title: Create the cluster
steps.description: Initialize the cluster with 3 masters and 3 replicas using redis-cli.
steps.warning: This redistributes all hash slots. Run on a fresh cluster or expect data to be reshuffled.

steps.title: Verify cluster status
steps.description: Check that all nodes joined the cluster and slots are assigned.

steps.title: Check cluster node roles
steps.description: List all nodes with their roles, slot ranges, and replication status.

steps.title: Test cluster operations
steps.description: Write and read data using cluster-aware mode in redis-cli.
steps.command: redis-cli -c -p 7001 SET mykey 'hello' && redis-cli -c -p 7001 GET mykey
steps.tip: The -c flag enables cluster mode in redis-cli, automatically following MOVED redirections.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Redis Cluster Setup (local development)
set -euo pipefail

PORTS=(7001 7002 7003 7004 7005 7006)
BASE_DIR="/tmp/redis-cluster"

echo "=== Preparing node directories ==="
for PORT in "${PORTS[@]}"; do
  mkdir -p "$BASE_DIR/$PORT"
  cat > "$BASE_DIR/$PORT/redis.conf" <<CONF
port $PORT
cluster-enabled yes
cluster-config-file nodes-$PORT.conf
cluster-node-timeout 5000
appendonly yes
dir $BASE_DIR/$PORT
CONF
done

echo ""
echo "=== Starting Redis nodes ==="
for PORT in "${PORTS[@]}"; do
  redis-server "$BASE_DIR/$PORT/redis.conf" --daemonize yes
  echo "Started node on port $PORT"
done

sleep 2

echo ""
echo "=== Creating cluster ==="
redis-cli --cluster create \
  127.0.0.1:7001 127.0.0.1:7002 127.0.0.1:7003 \
  127.0.0.1:7004 127.0.0.1:7005 127.0.0.1:7006 \
  --cluster-replicas 1 --cluster-yes

echo ""
echo "=== Cluster info ==="
redis-cli -p 7001 CLUSTER INFO

echo ""
echo "=== Cluster nodes ==="
redis-cli -p 7001 CLUSTER NODES

echo ""
echo "=== Testing read/write ==="
redis-cli -c -p 7001 SET test:key "cluster-works"
redis-cli -c -p 7001 GET test:key

faq.question: How many nodes do I need for a Redis Cluster?
faq.answer: A minimum of 3 master nodes is required. For high availability, add one replica per master (6 nodes total). This ensures the cluster survives one node failure per shard.

faq.question: Can I add nodes to a running cluster?
faq.answer: Yes. Use 'redis-cli --cluster add-node' to add a new node and 'redis-cli --cluster reshard' to rebalance hash slots to the new node without downtime.

faq.question: What are hash slots?
faq.answer: Redis Cluster divides the keyspace into 16384 hash slots. Each key is mapped to a slot via CRC16(key) mod 16384. Each master node owns a subset of slots, which determines where each key is stored.
